hook: The palace that was physically rotated 78° in 1970 — cut from its foundations and pivoted on rails over seven weeks so its colonnade would close the Saxon Axis instead of facing a market hall.
facts: Built around 1710 to a design attributed to Tylman van Gameren for the Radziwiłł family; bought by Aleksander Lubomirski in 1790 and rebuilt in classicist style 1791–1793 by Jakub Hempel.
Between 30 March and 18 May 1970 the whole palace (minus its demolished side wings) was turned about 78° around its eastern corner, sliding on steel lattice frames laid over tracks.
The rotation aligned the palace's colonnade with the main axis of the Saxon Garden (oś saska) and screened the modern Gwardia hall behind it.
The engineering operation was planned by Aleksander Mostowski; it remains the largest building of its kind ever moved in Poland.
It now houses the Business Centre Club, the Lubomirski Palace Press Centre and a private university.
w: In 1970 they cut this whole palace from its foundations and pivoted it 78 degrees on steel rails, swinging the colonnade over seven weeks to close the Saxon Axis and screen the market hall behind. Tylman built it around 1710; Aleksander Mostowski turned it. Still the largest building Poland has ever moved.

hook: The palace that lost its top floor as a punishment — after the National Gendarmerie hurled bombs at the tsarist viceroy Berg from its windows in 1863, Russian troops sacked it and threw Chopin's piano into the street.
facts: On 19 September 1863, members of the National Gendarmerie threw bombs from the palace's upper-floor windows at the carriage of Russian viceroy Fyodor Berg passing along Nowy Świat; Berg survived.
In reprisal, Russian soldiers plundered the building, its contents were dragged out toward the Copernicus monument and burned, and the palace was confiscated.
The piano of Fryderyk Chopin, kept in the flat of his sister Izabela Barcińska, was thrown from a window — an act commemorated by Cyprian Kamil Norwid's poem 'Fortepian Szopena'.
Owner Prince Stanisław Antoni Zamoyski was sentenced to hard labour and the palace's residents, including women and children, were arrested.
The building now houses several University of Warsaw faculties, including Journalism and Political Science.
w: The palace that lost its top floor as punishment. In 1863 gendarmes hurled bombs from these windows at the tsarist viceroy Berg; he survived, and Russian troops sacked the place in reprisal, burning its contents by the Copernicus monument. They threw Chopin's piano into the street -- the act Norwid mourned in 'Fortepian Szopena'.

hook: Corazzi's classicist 'temple of learning' that closes the vista of Krakowskie Przedmieście — built for the Society of Friends of Science, with Thorvaldsen's seated Copernicus out front.
facts: Built 1820–1823 by Antonio Corazzi for the Royal Society of Friends of Science (Towarzystwo Przyjaciół Nauk), on the site of a demolished late-Baroque Dominican church.
Bertel Thorvaldsen's bronze monument to Nicolaus Copernicus was unveiled in front of it on 11 May 1830, with Julian Ursyn Niemcewicz presiding.
The Society of Friends of Science was dissolved by tsarist authorities in 1832 as a reprisal after the failed November Uprising.
Restored to its classicist form in 1924–1926 by Marian Lalewicz; rebuilt again after wartime destruction in 1946–1950 under Piotr Biegański.
It is today the seat of the Polish Academy of Sciences (PAN).
w: Corazzi's classicist temple of learning, closing the long vista of Krakowskie Przedmiescie. Raised 1820-23 for the Society of Friends of Science -- dissolved by the tsar after the November Uprising -- it fronts Thorvaldsen's seated Copernicus, unveiled in 1830. Today the seat of the Polish Academy of Sciences.

hook: Tylman van Gameren's baroque masterpiece with Andreas Schlüter reliefs that turned the Krasiński family into descendants of ancient Rome — later the Supreme Court, now the National Library's treasure house.
facts: Built 1677–1683 (finished around 1695) by Tylman van Gameren for Jan Dobrogost Krasiński, with one of Warsaw's finest entre cour et jardin layouts.
Sculptor Andreas Schlüter carved the tympanum reliefs around 1689–1695, depicting the Krasiński family's claimed descent from the ancient Roman Corvinus line.
Sold to the state in 1765 as seat of the Crown Treasury Commission; under Congress Poland and again in 1917–1939 it housed the Supreme Court (Sąd Najwyższy).
About 85% destroyed by German forces during and after the 1944 Warsaw Uprising, then reconstructed 1948–1961.
Held by the National Library since 1958; in 2024 it reopened as the Palace of the Republic displaying rare collections.
legend: The Schlüter reliefs were designed to flatter the family with a fictitious lineage tracing the Krasińskis back to ancient Rome.
w: Tylman van Gameren's baroque masterpiece, its tympanum carved by Andreas Schluter around 1690 with reliefs flattering the Krasinskis as heirs of an ancient Roman line. It held the Supreme Court before 1939, burned in the Uprising, and reopened in 2024 as the National Library's treasure house. A fictitious Rome in stone.

hook: A neoclassical palace whose street balcony is carried by four straining stone Atlantes carved in 1787 — long one of the most admired facades on Krakowskie Przedmieście.
facts: Built from 1785 (finished 1792) by Stanisław Zawadzki for Lithuanian Field Hetman Ludwik Tyszkiewicz and his wife Konstancja Poniatowska, niece of King Stanisław August; interiors by Jan Chrystian Kamsetzer.
Four stone figures of Atlas supporting the first-floor balcony were carved in 1787 by royal sculptor André Le Brun.
August Potocki bought the palace from his mother in 1840, and it stayed in the Potocki family until 1923.
Acquired by Bank Gospodarstwa Krajowego in 1923, it housed the Polish Academy of Literature and rare National Library collections.
Deliberately bombed in the 1944 Uprising and rebuilt 1949–1956; it now houses the Museum of the University of Warsaw.
w: Four straining stone Atlantes, cut by the royal sculptor Andre Le Brun in 1787, shoulder the first-floor balcony -- long the most admired facade on Krakowskie Przedmiescie. Stanislaw Zawadzki built it for a Lithuanian hetman who married the king's niece; bombed in 1944, rebuilt, now the Museum of the University of Warsaw.

hook: The 'palace under the tin roof' — the first Warsaw building roofed in sheet copper, Prince Józef Poniatowski's legendary salon, now home to a renowned collection of oriental carpets.
facts: Rebuilt in late-Baroque style around 1720–1730 by Jakub Fontana, who added two wings and covered the building in sheet metal — the source of its name 'Pod Blachą' (under the tin).
From 1798 to 1813 it was the residence of Prince Józef Poniatowski, who lived there with his sister and the French countess Henrietta de Vauban; it became one of Warsaw's most famous social salons.
After Poniatowski became War Minister of the Duchy of Warsaw, the palace served as the headquarters of its army.
Transferred to the Royal Castle in 1989 and renovated in 2005–2008.
Its south wing now displays oriental carpets from the Teresa Sahakian Foundation collection, alongside rooms devoted to Prince Józef Poniatowski.
w: The palace under the tin -- the first Warsaw building roofed in sheet copper, hence the name. From 1798 Prince Jozef Poniatowski kept his legendary salon here, living with his sister and the French countess de Vauban; after he took the war ministry it ran the Duchy's army. Now a renowned collection of oriental carpets.

hook: A rococo palace stripped bare and turned into barracks — once richly ornamented, it became the home of the 'Czwartacy' 4th Infantry who won glory in the November Uprising.
facts: Built 1731–1746 in baroque style by Jan Zygmunt Deybel for Jan Fryderyk Sapieha, castellan of Troki and later chancellor of Lithuania.
Bought by the government in 1817 and rebuilt as barracks 1818–1820 by Wilhelm Henryk Minter, which stripped almost all of its rich rococo decoration from the facades.
From 1820 it housed the 4th Infantry Regiment, the famous 'Czwartacy' who distinguished themselves in the 1830–31 November Uprising; Russian troops then occupied it until 1915.
Destroyed in 1944 and reconstructed 1950–1955 under Maria Zachwatowicz.
Since 2005 it has housed a special-education centre for deaf children.
w: A rococo palace stripped bare and drilled into barracks. Deybel built it richly for the Sapiehas in the 1730s; in 1818 the government scraped the ornament off the facades and moved in the 4th Infantry -- the 'Czwartacy' who won glory in the November Uprising. Bombed in 1944, rebuilt, now a school for deaf children.

hook: Poland's grandest opera house, with one of the largest stages in the world behind Corazzi's neoclassical colonnade.
facts: The classicist building was raised 1825–1833 to a design by Antonio Corazzi (with Ludwik Kozubowski); the first performance was Rossini's Barber of Seville on 24 February 1833.
Its main stage covers roughly 1,150 m² and rises about 35 m, counted among the largest operatic stages in the world; the auditorium seats 1,768.
Gutted and dynamited by the Germans during and after the 1944 Uprising, it was rebuilt and expanded by Bohdan Pniewski and ceremonially reopened on 19 November 1965.
The first Polish-language opera, Maciej Kamieński's 'Nędza uszczęśliwiona', had premiered in Warsaw back in 1778, decades before this building existed.
w: Behind Corazzi's neoclassical colonnade hides one of the largest stages on earth -- roughly 1,150 square metres, rising some 35 metres into the flies. Raised 1825-33, opened with Rossini's Barber of Seville, dynamited by the Germans after the 1944 Uprising and rebuilt vast by Pniewski, reopening in 1965. Poland's grandest opera house.

hook: The National Philharmonic, opened in 1901 by Paderewski and home stage of the International Chopin Piano Competition.
facts: Built 1900–1901 to Karol Kozłowski's design; the inaugural concert on 5 November 1901 was conducted by Emil Młynarski with Ignacy Jan Paderewski as soloist.
It hosted the first three International Chopin Piano Competitions (1927, 1932, 1937) and has been the Competition's home ever since.
The building was destroyed in the Second World War and nearly half the orchestra's musicians were killed; it was rebuilt by 1955.
The reopened hall was inaugurated with the 5th International Chopin Competition (21 February – 20 March 1955), with 74 pianists from 25 countries.
w: Paderewski himself played the opening night here in November 1901, and ever since this hall has held the home stage of the International Chopin Piano Competition. War levelled it and killed nearly half the orchestra; rebuilt by 1955, it reopened with the fifth Competition -- 74 pianists from 25 countries. Where careers are still made.

hook: Stalin's 'gift to the Polish nation' — a 237 m socialist-realist tower whose 30th-floor terrace gives Warsaw's best panorama.
facts: Built 1952–1955 to a design by Soviet architect Lev Rudnev, inspired by Moscow's 'Seven Sisters'; it was handed over for use on 22 July 1955.
On completion it stood 230.68 m; a 1994 antenna mast raised the total height to 237 m, long Poland's tallest building.
The public observation terrace on the 30th floor sits at 114 m and offers a 360-degree panorama of the city.
A wave of suicide leaps began from that terrace in 1956 — first a Frenchman, then seven Poles — after which it was glazed in.
legend: Locals nickname it variously 'the Russian wedding cake', 'Stalin's syringe' and 'Pekin', and joke that the terrace offers the finest view in Warsaw precisely because it is the one spot from which you can't see the Palace itself.
w: Stalin's 'gift to the Polish nation', 237 metres of socialist-realist wedding cake the city has never quite forgiven. Rudnev modelled it on Moscow's Seven Sisters, 1952-55. The 30th-floor terrace at 114 metres still gives the best panorama in town -- the joke runs that it's the one view that omits the Palace itself.

a: Pałac Kultury i Nauki, wejście od Emilii Plater, Śródmieście
hook: The 3,000-seat concert hall inside the Palace of Culture where the Rolling Stones played behind the Iron Curtain in 1967.
facts: The Sala Kongresowa occupies the western wing of the Palace of Culture and Science (separate entrance from ul. Emilii Plater), with an official capacity around 2,880–3,000.
On 13 April 1967 the Rolling Stones played two shows here — among the first major Western rock concerts staged behind the Iron Curtain.
Some 10,000 fans, including arrivals from Czechoslovakia and East Germany, massed outside; mounted militia and tear gas were used against those without tickets.
The hall has been closed for renovation since 2014.
legend: Legend holds the band were paid partly in vodka and that the support act Czerwono-Czarni were drowned out by fans, though chronicles note the Stones' own sets were cut short by the hall's notoriously bad acoustics.
sources: https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pa%C5%82ac_Kultury_i_Nauki (Sala Kongresowa inside the Palace, ~3,000 seats, closed since 2014; building coordinates confirm location)
w: The 3,000-seat hall inside the Palace of Culture where the Rolling Stones played behind the Iron Curtain in April 1967 -- two shows, while 10,000 fans massed outside and the militia answered with tear gas. The story goes the band took part of their fee in vodka; the acoustics, all agree, cut their sets short. Shuttered since 2014.

hook: An eternal flame under the last surviving arcade of the vanished Saxon Palace, guarded around the clock.
facts: Unveiled on 2 November 1925 beneath the central arcade of the Saxon Palace; the entombed remains were brought from the Defenders of Lwów cemetery.
The tomb was designed by sculptor Stanisław Kazimierz Ostrowski.
Germans blew up the Saxon Palace at the war's end, but the tomb was rebuilt and re-unveiled in 1946 within the surviving fragment of the colonnade.
An eternal flame burns here and a permanent honour guard from the Representative Battalion of the Polish Army stands watch, changed ceremonially on state holidays.
sources: https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gr%C3%B3b_Nieznanego_%C5%BBo%C5%82nierza_w_Warszawie (1925 unveiling, Ostrowski, 1946 rebuild, honour guard; coordinates 52°14′27″N 21°00′41″E)
w: An eternal flame burns under the last surviving arcade of a vanished palace. The Saxon Palace framed this tomb in 1925, holding remains brought from the defenders of Lwow; the Germans blew the palace to rubble, but the colonnade fragment and its flame were raised again in 1946. A guard stands watch over it, day and night.

hook: Warsaw's first public park, a 15-hectare Baroque garden opened to ordinary townspeople in 1727.
facts: On 27 May 1727 King August II opened the garden to the residents of Warsaw, making it the first public city park in Poland.
The park covers about 15.5 hectares.
Twenty-one Baroque sandstone statues of muses, virtues and seasons — mainly by Jan Jerzy Plersch — survive from the Saxon era.
Its monumental Great Fountain, designed by Henryk Marconi and inaugurated in 1855, was Warsaw's first permanent public fountain; a marble sundial of 1863 stands nearby.
sources: https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ogr%C3%B3d_Saski_w_Warszawie (1727 public opening, 15.5 ha, statues, Marconi fountain; coordinates 52°14′25″N 21°00′30″E)
https://zzw.waw.pl/nasze-tereny/parki/ogrod-saski/ (city greenery board page, first public park, fountain and sundial)
w: Warsaw's first public park, thrown open to ordinary townspeople by August II on a May day in 1727 -- long before kings shared their gardens. Some 15 hectares of Baroque green, scattered with 21 sandstone muses and virtues by Plersch, and Marconi's Great Fountain of 1855, the city's first. A marble sundial keeps the old time nearby.

hook: Warsaw's oldest secular monument and unofficial emblem — a king on a 22-metre column on Castle Square.
facts: Raised 1643–1644 on the funding of King Władysław IV to honour his father Sigismund III Vasa, designed by Augustyn Locci and Constantino Tencalla.
It was set up before the Royal Castle on 24 November 1644.
Figure, plinth and pedestal together stand about 22 m tall.
It was Warsaw's first secular monument and quickly became a symbol of the city; toppled in 1944, it was re-erected after the war.
legend: Tradition holds that should the sabre in King Sigismund's bronze hand ever fall, disaster will strike the city — a saying revived when the column was felled during the war.
sources: https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kolumna_Zygmunta_III_Wazy (1643–44 build, Locci/Tencalla, 24 Nov 1644, 22 m, first secular monument; coordinates ~52.2470, 21.0134)
https://zabytek.pl/en/obiekty/g-225713 (heritage register entry, Plac Zamkowy location)
w: Warsaw's oldest secular monument -- a bronze king on a 22-metre column, set before the Castle in 1644 by his son Wladyslaw IV. Toppled in 1944, re-raised after the war. The old saying warns that should the sabre ever slip from Sigismund's hand, disaster falls on the city -- a line revived the day the column came down.

hook: Warsaw's great Art-Nouveau Chopin, brooding beneath a windswept bronze willow, with free Sunday recitals at his feet.
facts: Designed by Wacław Szymanowski in flowing Art Nouveau (secesja) style, showing Chopin under a stylised Mazovian weeping willow; it was unveiled on 14 November 1926 in Łazienki Park near the Belweder.
On 31 May 1940 the German occupiers blew the statue up and cut it apart with torches — among the very first Warsaw monuments they destroyed.
Recast from preserved documentation, it was re-unveiled on 11 May 1958.
Since 1959 free open-air Chopin piano recitals have been held at the monument every Sunday from May to September.
sources: https://pl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pomnik_Fryderyka_Chopina_w_Warszawie (Szymanowski, 1926 unveiling, 1940 destruction, 1958 rebuild, Sunday concerts; coordinates 52°12′53″N 21°01′41″E)
w: Chopin broods beneath a windswept bronze willow, Szymanowski's great Art Nouveau monument in Lazienki. The Germans torched and dismembered it in 1940 -- among the first Warsaw statues they destroyed -- and it was recast from old drawings in 1958. Every summer Sunday since 1959, free piano recitals play at his feet.

hook: A double-deck steel bridge where trams and bikes rumble on the lower level while cars cross above — and a parallel railway span links the city to the zoo.
facts: Built 1957–1959 to a design by Janusz Ratyński, on the piers of an older bridge.
The original combined rail-and-road bridge stood here from 1873–1875 until the Germans blew it up on 13 September 1944.
It is a two-level bridge: four road lanes plus a pavement on top, two tram tracks, a pavement and a cycle path below.
A separate railway span carries two tracks linking Warszawa Gdańska and Warszawa ZOO stations.
It was named Most Gdański at the inauguration of the whole Route of Bridges (Trasa Mostowa) on 21 July 1959.
w: A double-deck steel bridge: cars cross up top while trams, cyclists and walkers rumble along the level below. Built 1957-59 on the piers of an 1875 crossing the Germans dropped in 1944, it opened the whole Route of Bridges in July 1959. A separate railway span beside it still carries the line out to the zoo.

hook: A late-1990s temple of justice ringed by columns that are literally inscribed with Latin legal maxims — Roman law you can read while walking past.
facts: The Supreme Court has occupied the complex at plac Krasińskich 2/4/6 since 1999.
Designed by Marek Budzyński with Zbigniew Badowski and a team of cooperating architects — Budzyński also designed the University of Warsaw Library with its text motifs.
Its colonnade carries 86 legal maxims (paremiae) in Latin with Polish translations and source notes.
The maxims were selected by a team of Roman-law scholars led by Prof. Witold Wołodkiewicz of the University of Warsaw, drawn mainly from the Digest and Code of Justinian.
Three caryatids stand at the building, part of its allegorical sculptural programme.
w: A temple of justice ringed by columns you can literally read: 86 Latin legal maxims, each with a Polish gloss, run around the colonnade -- Roman law from the Digest, chosen by scholars under Witold Wolodkiewicz. Marek Budzynski designed it, as he did the university library with its lettered walls. Caryatids and all, opened 1999.

hook: Built from bricks levied across society to house the Communist Party — and in 1991 it became, fittingly, the home of Poland's first stock exchange.
facts: Erected 1948–1952 at Nowy Świat 6/12 near rondo de Gaulle'a, funded by compulsory 'brick' contributions collected from the public.
Designed by architects Wacław Kłyszewski, Jerzy Mokrzyński and Eugeniusz Wierzbicki.
Intended for the leadership of several parties (hence 'Dom Partii'), it became the seat of the PZPR Central Committee after the PPR–PPS merger.
From 1991 to 2000 it housed the Warsaw Stock Exchange, whose first session took place on 16 April 1991.
The building was entered into the register of monuments in 2009.
w: Raised 1948-52 from 'bricks' levied across society to house the Communist Party's Central Committee -- and then, in a turn nobody scripted, it took in Poland's first stock exchange. The opening session traded here in April 1991, capitalism dealing from the old PZPR headquarters. Listed as a monument since 2009.

hook: A socialist-realist palace on its own artificial hill, crowned with a green dome — Moscow's deliberate claim to a strategic spot in the capital.
facts: Built 1954–1955 at Belwederska 49 for the embassy of the Soviet Union; today it houses the embassy of the Russian Federation.
Designed by Soviet architects Aleksander Wielikanow and Igor Rożyn.
It takes the form of a palace with side wings, raised on an artificial elevation with wide staircases and a dominating dome.
The front facing Belwederska is about one hundred metres wide.
It is a socialist-realist work referencing 18th–19th-century Russian classical palace residences, from the early Cold War period.
w: A socialist-realist palace raised on its own artificial hill, green dome and hundred-metre front facing Belwederska -- Moscow's deliberate claim to a strategic patch of the capital. Wielikanow and Rozyn built it 1954-55 for the Soviet embassy, all wide staircases and borrowed tsarist grandeur. The Russian flag flies there still.

hook: A 220-metre glass spire on a public square — for years the tallest office tower in Warsaw, its twin-curved facade a landmark of the new Wola skyline.
facts: The complex was built 2011–2016 and officially opened on 12 May 2016 in the Wola district, addressed to plac Europejski.
Its tower reaches 220 m to the tips of its spires (180 m to the roof), long the tallest office building in the city.
Designed by the Belgian studio Jaspers-Eyers Architects with the Polish-Belgian PROJEKT studio.
Two lower 55-metre curved, glazed buildings flank it, with the public plac Europejski laid out between them by Wirtz International.
It was developed by Ghelamco Poland and sold in June 2019.
w: A 220-metre glass spire rising over a public square, its twin-curved facade for years the tallest office tower in Warsaw. Ghelamco and the Belgian studio Jaspers-Eyers built it 2011-16, flanked by two low 55-metre wings with plac Europejski laid out between them. The new Wola skyline starts here.
